metadata.title: Research Chronic Inflammation and Cancer with Claude: Why a Wound That Never Heals Becomes a Tumor
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Rudolf Virchow saw immune cells in tumors in 1863 and proposed that cancer arises from chronic irritation. His colleagues ignored him. A hundred and fifty years later, the molecular biology proved him right — in precise detail.

Chronic inflammation contributes to approximately 20% of all human cancers. Four molecular mechanisms link persistent inflammation to carcinogenesis: DNA damage via reactive oxygen and nitrogen species, proliferation pressure from repeated regeneration cycles, pro-tumor cytokine signaling through IL-6/STAT3 and TNF-α/NF-κB axes, and immune dysregulation toward tolerance. Each mechanism has a clinical proof of principle — a case where treating the inflammatory driver reduces cancer risk.
Ask Claude to map the four mechanisms.

props.command: claude "Research the four molecular mechanisms by which chronic inflammation promotes carcinogenesis: DNA damage from reactive oxygen/nitrogen species, proliferation pressure from repeated regeneration cycles, pro-tumor cytokine signaling (IL-6/STAT3 and TNF-α/NF-κB), and immune dysregulation toward tolerance. For each mechanism, provide one concrete clinical example where treating the inflammatory driver reduces cancer risk, with supporting evidence."

props.code:
# inflammation_cancer_brief.md  --  Claude output

## 1. ROS/RNS DNA Damage
- Mechanism: oxidative DNA lesions → mutations
- Clinical proof: H. pylori eradication reduces gastric cancer

## 2. Proliferation Pressure
- Mechanism: repeated regeneration cycles → replication errors
- Clinical proof: HBV vaccination reduces hepatocellular carcinoma

## 3. IL-6/STAT3 + TNF-α/NF-κB Signaling
- Mechanism: cytokine axes drive pro-tumor gene expression
- Clinical proof: IBD management reduces colorectal cancer risk

## 4. Immune Dysregulation
- Mechanism: chronic inflammation shifts to immune tolerance
- Clinical proof: HCV cure with DAAs reduces HCC incidence ~70%

beats.narration_text: Brief generated: 4 mechanisms, each with molecular basis, clinical example, and evidence level. H. pylori eradication: published RCT data showing 40% reduction in gastric cancer in high-risk populations. HCV cure: DAA treatment reduces HCC incidence by approximately 70% in cirrhotic patients. Low-dose aspirin colorectal data included with bleeding risk trade-off flagged. IL-6/STAT3 and TNF-α/NF-κB correctly identified as primary cytokine axes.

props.command: claude "Build the clinical case for or against long-term low-dose aspirin for a 55-year-old with colorectal cancer family history — include mechanistic rationale, RCT evidence, and bleeding risk trade-off."
props.runningText: weighing aspirin prevention vs. bleeding risk...

beats.narration_text: Aspirin case analysis: Mechanistic rationale strong — COX-2 inhibition reduces prostaglandin-driven proliferation and reduces IL-6. Evidence: 3 RCTs show 20-30% colorectal cancer risk reduction at low dose. Contraindications: GI bleeding risk increases 1.5-2x at 75mg/day; major bleeding risk 0.3% per year. Decision: Current evidence does not support universal recommendation — USPSTF recommends only for cardiovascular benefit with colorectal as secondary benefit in ages 50-59. The aspirin question is unresolved because the same mechanism that prevents cancer also increases bleeding risk.

Virchow's 1863 observation is now a therapeutic principle. If the inflammatory process is the carcinogen, treating the process prevents the cancer. H. pylori eradication, HBV vaccination, and HCV cure are the three proven proof-of-principle interventions. Aspirin remains contested because the same mechanism that suppresses cancer also suppresses hemostasis.
Next: research the cancer stem cell debate — one experiment said only 1 in 10,000 cells can restart a cancer. Another said almost every cell can.
Nik Bear Brown. Research it with Claude. At nikbearbrown.com.
